units.title: Container Element (div)
units.summary: The HTML div element is used to define a container that groups other HTML elements together.
units.source_span:
The HTML `div` element defines a container. ```html

Tourism

Plan your trip wherever you want to go

```

explanation.text: The HTML `div` element is a container—a box that holds and groups other HTML elements together. You use it to organize related content like headings, paragraphs, and buttons in one place, making it easier to style or manage them as a unit.

analogy.text: A div is like a cardboard box that holds different items together. The box itself (the div) is like the container, and the items inside it—a heading, paragraph, and button—are like the grouped HTML elements. Just as the box keeps related items in one place, the div groups related HTML elements together.
analogy.grounding_check: Box = 'container' (source: 'div element defines a container'); items inside (heading, paragraph, button) = 'other HTML elements' (source: example shows h1, p, button grouped inside div)

scenarios.text: You are building a tourism website and want to group a heading, description, and button together so they stay visually connected. You wrap all three inside a `div` to keep them organized.

code_playground.html:
Tourism

Plan your trip wherever you want to go

questions.question: What is the main purpose of the HTML div element?
options: To define a container that groups other HTML elements together
To create a line break between paragraphs
To display text in bold formatting
To add a border around a single element
questions.explanation: The source states that the div element 'defines a container' used to group other HTML elements together. The other options describe different HTML elements or features, not the div's purpose.

questions.question: In the example, which elements are grouped inside the div container?
options: An h1 heading, a paragraph, and a button
Only the h1 heading and the paragraph
The div and the h1 heading only
questions.explanation: The source example shows an h1, a p, and a button all nested inside the div tags. These three elements are the ones grouped together by the container.

questions.question: What does the div element do with the elements inside it?
questions.explanation: The source explicitly states the div 'defines a container that groups other HTML elements together.' Grouping is the core function; the other options are misconceptions about what a container does.

questions.question: Based on the example, can a div container hold different types of elements?
options: Yes, it holds headings, paragraphs, and buttons together
No, it can only hold paragraphs
No, it can only hold one type of element
Yes, but only text elements, not buttons
questions.explanation: The source example demonstrates a div containing an h1, a p, and a button—three different element types. This shows that a div can group multiple different kinds of elements.

units.title: CSS Rule Syntax
units.summary: CSS rules consist of a selector followed by a block containing one or more property-value pairs.

explanation.text: A CSS rule is a block of styling instructions. It starts with a selector (the part that picks which elements to style), then has curly braces containing one or more property-value pairs separated by semicolons. Each property is the thing you want to change (like color or size), and the value is what you want it to be.

analogy.text: A CSS rule is like a mailing label on a package. The address on the label is like the selector—it identifies which package (element) the rule applies to. The contents listed inside the box are like the property-value pairs—each item (property) has a specific quantity or description (value) that describes what's in the package.
analogy.grounding_check: selector → 'selector' in source; property-value pairs → 'property1: value1; property2: value2' in source

scenarios.text: CSS rules consist of a selector followed by a block containing one or more property-value pairs.

questions.question: What two main parts make up a CSS rule?
options: A selector and a declaration block
A property and a value only
A class name and a tag name
An ID and a color code
questions.explanation: The source shows a CSS rule has a selector (like the word before the curly braces) followed by a block with property-value pairs inside curly braces. The other options name only parts of a rule or unrelated concepts.

questions.question: Inside the curly braces of a CSS rule, what do you write?
options: Just the color and font size
questions.explanation: The source span shows that inside the curly braces (the declaration block) there are property-value pairs separated by semicolons. The other options either name things outside the braces or incorrectly limit what can go inside.

questions.question: How should each property-value pair in a CSS rule end?
questions.explanation: The source span shows each property-value pair ends with a semicolon (for example, `property1: value1;`). A colon separates the property from the value, but the semicolon ends the pair.

questions.question: In the CSS rule shown, what comes immediately after the selector?
questions.explanation: The source span shows the selector is followed directly by an opening curly brace `{`, which begins the declaration block. The other options are parts that appear later or in different contexts.

units.title: Text Align Property
units.summary: The CSS text-align property controls the horizontal alignment of text within an HTML element, with options for center, left, or right alignment.
units.source_span:
The CSS `text-align` property specifies the horizontal alignment of the text in an HTML element.

```css
.h-center {
  text-align: center;
}
```

| Value  | Description                   |
| ------ | ----------------------------- |
| center | Aligns the text to the center |
| left   | Aligns the text to the left   |
| right  | Aligns the text to the right  |

explanation.text: The CSS `text-align` property controls where text lines up horizontally inside an HTML element. You can choose to push text to the center, left edge, or right edge of its container. This property only affects the horizontal (left-to-right) direction.

analogy.text: Imagine text as books on a shelf. The `text-align` property is like deciding where to push those books: `text-align: left` pushes them to the left edge of the shelf (just like "Aligns the text to the left"), `text-align: center` stacks them in the middle (just like "Aligns the text to the center"), and `text-align: right` pushes them to the right edge (just like "Aligns the text to the right"). The shelf itself is the HTML element that contains the text.
analogy.grounding_check: left/center/right alignment values trace to "Aligns the text to the left/center/right"; the shelf traces to "the text in an HTML element"; the pushing action traces to "specifies the horizontal alignment"

scenarios.text: You're building a website header and want the title text centered on the page. You apply `text-align: center;` to the heading element so the text appears in the middle instead of at the left edge.

code_playground.html:
Welcome to My Site

This text is centered horizontally

Left-aligned heading

This text is aligned to the left

Right-aligned heading

This text is aligned to the right

questions.question: What does the CSS text-align property control?
options: The horizontal alignment of text within an element
The vertical alignment of text within an element
The size of the text in an element
The color of the text in an element
questions.explanation: The source states that text-align specifies the horizontal alignment of text. The other options describe different CSS properties (vertical alignment, font size, and color), not what text-align does.

questions.question: Which value would you use with text-align to move text to the middle of an element?
questions.explanation: The source table shows that 'center' aligns text to the center. 'Middle' is not a valid text-align value; the other options are made-up property names.

questions.question: What is the correct CSS syntax to align text to the left?
questions.explanation: The source shows that 'left' is a valid value for the text-align property. The other options use incorrect property names or incorrect value syntax.

questions.question: According to the source, how many horizontal alignment options does text-align have?
options: Four: center, left, right, and justify
Five: center, left, right, top, and bottom
questions.explanation: The source table lists exactly three values: center, left, and right. The other options either omit one of these or add values not mentioned in the source.
